coredata.dc:title: White light-driven enhanced cerium-doped carbon dots activity to combat multidrug-resistant bacterial infection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Na
Zhang, Lijun
Wang, Mengzhen
Liu, Lin
Huang, Chengzhi
Zhan, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Infections caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria are increasing and becoming an urgent global health crisis. The discovery and development of novel antibacterial agents to combat MDR are highly desirable. Here, we report the fabrication of cerium-doped carbon dots (CeCDs) with a simple hydrothermal method, which exhibit intrinsic broad efficacy against MDR bacteria including clinical isolates while maintaining low cytotoxicity and hemolytic effects. Importantly, the antibacterial activity of CeCDs is dramatically improved owing to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon white light irradiation. Comprehensive analyses revealed that the CeCDs can penetrate the bacterial wall, disrupt the cell membrane, and prevent the biofilm formation, possibly hindering the bacterial resistance development. And the interaction of CeCDs with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) may contribute to the higher activity against Gram-negative bacteria strains. The treatment of CeCDs in a murine skin infection model can significantly reduce the number of bacteria on infected sites and accelerate wound healing by irradiation with light. Overall, CeCDs show great promise as low-cost and efficient antibacterial agents for chronic wounds and may be served as a powerful weapon to fight against the growing threat of MDR bacterial infection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ce-doped carbon dots
Light-enhanced effect
Photodynamic therapy
Multidrug-resistant bacteria

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photophysical and Photochemical properties of CeCDs
Antibacterial activity of CeCDs in vitro
Applications in antibacterial therapy in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21976144 and 22134005), and Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0179) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CeCDs. (a) AFM image and corresponding height profile of the linescan. (b) TEM image of CeCDs (insets: HRTEM image and size distribution). (c) FTIR spectra of CeCDs and CDs. (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of CeCDs. Inset is a photograph of the CeCDs irradiated by 365 nm UV light.

Photophysical and Photochemical properties of CeCDs. (a) Absolute quantum yield of CeCDs and CDs. (b) Transient photocurrents response of CDs and CeCDs. (c) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of CDs and CeCDs. (d) ROS production based on DCFH-DA fluorescence intensity. (e) Electron spin resonance spectra of ·OH, O2·–, 1O2, and h + generated by CeCDs in the dark or light irradiation.

Antibacterial performance of CeCDs. (a) Plate photographs of E. coli, S. aureus, ESBL-Ec, and MRSA with different concentrations of CeCDs treatment under dark or light irradiation. (b, c, d, e) Statistical analysis of bacterial viability after treatment with CeCDs. **P < 0.01, *P < 0.05.

Possible antibacterial mechanism and binding of CeCDs to bacteria. (a,b) SEM and CLSM images of E. coli, S. aureus, ESBL-Ec, and MRSA staining in different treatment groups. (c) Confocal images of the membrane permeability analysis of ESBL-Ec and MRSA stained by CeCDs. (d) Bacterial viability after treatment with different concentration of NAC under white light. (e) Bacterial viability of E. coli after treatment with different concentration of LPS. (f) Quantitative analysis of the E. coli and S. aureus biofilm inhibition based on crystal violet staining results.

Evaluating the therapeutic performance of CeCDs in the healing of chronic wounds infected with E. coli. (a) Schematic representation of the mouse skin wound infection-healing model. (b) Photographs of CeCDs-treated E. coli-infected wounds from day 0 to day 13 (n = 3). (c) Statistical analysis of wound healing rate in each group. (d) Schematic diagram of wound shrinkage after CeCDs treatment from day 0 to day 13. (e) Representative H&E staining images of wound tissue with different treatments on day 13. Scale bar: 200 μm.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CeCDs and their applications in antibacterial treatment enhanced with white light. Gram-negative bacteria (G-), Gram-positive bacteria (G+).

head.ce:title: White light-driven enhanced cerium-doped carbon dots activity to combat multidrug-resistant bacterial infection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics, Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics, Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analytics, Chongqing Science and Technology Bureau, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Laboratory Medicine, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, The Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400010, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Infections caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria are increasing and becoming an urgent global health crisis. The discovery and development of novel antibacterial agents to combat MDR are highly desirable. Here, we report the fabrication of cerium-doped carbon dots (CeCDs) with a simple hydrothermal method, which exhibit intrinsic broad efficacy against MDR bacteria including clinical isolates while maintaining low cytotoxicity and hemolytic effects. Importantly, the antibacterial activity of CeCDs is dramatically improved owing to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon white light irradiation. Comprehensive analyses revealed that the CeCDs can penetrate the bacterial wall, disrupt the cell membrane, and prevent the biofilm formation, possibly hindering the bacterial resistance development. And the interaction of CeCDs with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) may contribute to the higher activity against Gram-negative bacteria strains. The treatment of CeCDs in a murine skin infection model can significantly reduce the number of bacteria on infected sites and accelerate wound healing by irradiation with light. Overall, CeCDs show great promise as low-cost and efficient antibacterial agents for chronic wounds and may be served as a powerful weapon to fight against the growing threat of MDR bacterial infection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Infections caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria has become a top threat to global public’s health. As Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) reported, more than 2 million resistant infections and 23,000 deaths occur annually in the United States alone. Especially during the COVID-19 pandemic, longer hospitals stays and higher mortality with drug-resistant pathogens coinfections were well-documented [1]. There were an estimated 4.95 million deaths associated with bacterial antimicrobial resistance in 2019, including 1.27 million deaths directly attributable to resistance. In addition, according to the World Health Organization (WHO), the development of new antibacterial medicines is insufficient to tackle the challenge of increasing emergence and continued spread of multidrug-resistance as well as the most of current market-existing antibiotics will fail to treat deadly infectious diseases in the future [2]. Therefore, it is urgently necessary to explore new strategies with high antibacterial activity to prevent antibiotic-resistant threats.

To date, multiple nanomaterial-based novel systems endowed with high antimicrobial activities have emerged to address MDR infections [3], such as direct damage [4], photothermal therapy (PTT) [5], photodynamic therapy (PDT) [6], sonodynamic therapy (SDT) [7], and combination therapy [8]. Among them, PDT utilizing appropriate light-driven photosensitizers to produce cytotoxic reactive oxygen species, which can oxidatively damage the major biological components (lipids, proteins, and nucleic acid) of bacteria and in turn cause bacteria death, has been demonstrated to be an ideal antimicrobial method because of the non-invasive nature, broad antibacterial spectrum, less acquired resistance, and minimal side effects [9,10]. Various types of nanomaterial-based photosensitizers including fullerene [11], PCN-224(Zr/Ti) [12], TiO2 [13], upconversion nanoparticles [14], Ag2S@WS2 [15], and black phosphorus [16] have been designed and developed for MDR bacterial infections with the activation of UV light, visible light, or NIR light [17]. Each type of these light sources has its advantages. White light and visible light as excitation light for photodynamic therapy has the advantages of economic availability, high applicability and very low toxicity [18]. However, it is well known that the UV light is harmful to living organisms while the utility of NIR is generally limited by the relative high cost and requirement of trained operators. Thus, novel nano-photosensitizers with high ROS-generation and excellent biocompatibility which manipulated by convenient and efficient light is highly desirable.

Carbon dots, an emerging class of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, have gained considerable attention in photomedicine because of their high photoluminescence efficiency, controllable surface chemistry, excellent biocompatibility, good water solubility and thermal/chemical stability [19]. Han et al. found that graphene quantum dots could be served as an efficient PDT agent for cancer therapy [20]. To further improve the physicochemical properties of carbon dots, more attention has recently focused on the heteroatom doping. For example, Huang et al. obtained the ultra-stable tellurium-doped carbon quantum dots, which can be applied for cell protection and photodynamic therapy [21]. Qu et al. have reported the amplified ROS generation ability upon choosing chlorine as the dopant, exhibiting significant antibacterial activity [22]. It is noteworthy that the antibacterial activities of heteroatom co-doped carbon dots can be further promoted upon exposure to light irradiation [23–25].

Herein, we hypothesized that cerium-doped carbon dots (CeCDs) with photodynamic activity could be effective for preventing and controlling MDR bacterial infection ( Scheme 1). Cerium, one of the rare-earth elements, has been widely used as an attractive dopant in a variety of functional nanomaterials due to its superior biosafety and distinctive physicochemical properties, especially the unique interconversion between the valence states of Ce(III) and Ce(IV) [26,27]. CeCDs was prepared by a facile hydrothermal method, using citric acid, ethanediamine, and Ce(NO3)3·6 H2O as precursors. Interestingly, the as-prepared CeCDs can effectively penetrate and destroy the cell membrane and subsequently kill bacteria, showing broad-spectrum antibacterial activities against MDR bacteria strains while exhibiting low toxicity towards the red blood cells (RBCs) and is insusceptible to develop resistance. More importantly, the antibacterial activity was greatly enhanced through the generation of abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS) under white light irradiation. It also can effectively eliminate bacteria in a murine superficial skin wound infection model and displayed no acute dermal toxicity. Overall, CeCDs can be served as potent photosensitizer and have great potential as therapeutic agents against serious clinical MDR bacterial infections.

Typically, for the preparation of CeCDs, CA (531 mg) and Ce(NO3)3‧6 H2O (36.6 mg) were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL) and stirred for 1 h at room temperature. Subsequently, EDA (55.5 μL) was added into the mixture and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 4 h in an air oven. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove the large particles and the brown supernatant was then filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The crude products were purified by silica gel column with methanol and dichloromethane as the eluate. Finally, CeCDs were obtained via freeze-drying and stored at 4 ºC for further use. CDs were prepared in the same manner except without the addition of Ce(NO3)3‧6 H2O.

E. coli, S. aureus, and MDR clinical isolated (ESBL-Ec, MRSA) were grown overnight in Luria Bertani (LB) medium, which was self-made in laboratory. In brief, tryptone (10 g), yeast extract (5 g), and NaCl (10 g) were dissolved with tap water and adjusted the pH value of the mixture to around 7.4. Then, the mixture solution was diluted to 1 L and sterilized before use. Bacteria was cultured in the shaker at 37 ºC overnight and collected the cells in mid exponential phase. Note that each repetition was started with picking individual colonies from LB solid medium.

Subsequently, four different bacteria strains were used for evaluating the antibacterial ability of CeCDs in vivo. In brief, bacteria with 105 CFU/mL were added to 96-well plates followed by incubation with different concentration of CeCDs (0, 5, 10, 20, and 40 μg/mL). After conducting white light (3613 lux) irradiation for 2 h, the bacteria were spread onto the solid agar plate and grown for 18–24 h at 37 ºC. The survival rate was calculated by the percentage of the colony numbers after being treated by CeCDs with light irradiation to that of bacteria number in the control. Bacteria incubation with CeCDs without irradiation were also tested under the same conditions. The experiments on MDR bacteria were carried out in the Second Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University.

All animal experiments were approved by the Experimental Animal Ethics Committee of Southwest University (ethical number: IACUC-20231023–07) and carried out in accordance with the guidelines of the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of the National Institutes of Health. In brief, an incision wound with an E. coli-infected was established on the back of the mice. CeCDs solution (2.5 mg/kg) was then added to each wound site every day, followed by 2 h white light irradiation. The bacterial cells treated with saline was used as the control. Photos of the infected wounds were captured at different time points, and the whole wound healing process was defined as 13 days. And bacteria in the wound tissue of mice was inoculated and counted by plate culture method.

CeCDs was easily achieved by a bottom-up method that using CA, cerium nitrate and ethylenediamine as precursor under hydrothermal conditions, followed by purification with silica-gel column chromatography. TEM image certifies the formation of nearly spherical CeCDs with an average particle size of 3.75 nm ( Fig. 1b), which is consistent with the AFM observation (a typical height of 3.7–3.8 nm) (Fig. 1a). Representative high-resolution TEM analysis demonstrates that CeCDs contains well-resolved crystal lattice with an interlayer distance of 0.21 nm, which is ascribed to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [28]. The degree of crystallinity was further confirmed by Raman spectra, in which the crystalline G band at 1585 cm−1 is stronger than the disordered D band at 1326 cm−1, with the intensity ratio (I D/I G) of 0.93 (Fig. S1).

In the FTIR spectra of CeCDs (Fig. 1C), the strong bands at 1658, 1558, and 1045 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of C = O, N-H, and C−O, respectively, while the broad peak centered at 3402 cm−1 is associated with O−H/N−H bonding. The absorptions at 1415 cm−1 and 2989 cm−1 can be related to the the presence of the bending vibration and stretching vibration of C-H, respectively. In addition, XPS surface analysis was employed to investigate the chemical composition of CeCDs. The full survey of XPS spectrum CeCDs reveals the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) atoms (Fig. S2). From the deconvoluted C 1 s XPS spectrum, three peaks ascribed to C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N (286.1 eV), and CO (287.9 eV) bonds were observed. The N 1 s peak consisted of two bands at 399.5 and 400.4 eV, which correspond to Amide N and Pyrrolic/Amino N, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 530.5, 531.1, and 532.1 eV, which represents the CO, O–CO, and C–OH groups, respectively. FTIR and XPS measurements confirm that there are multiple oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups etc. on the surface of CeCDs. Though the presence of Ce species was not identified by XPS, Ce amount in the prepared CeCDs was assessed by inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) using the standard curve method (Fig. S3). Strikingly, although metal ions play a crucial role in the synthesis of CDs with varied QYs, they are almost absent in the formed CDs, that is, the obtained CDs are metal-free, and the metal ions play a role similar to a“catalyst” during the CD formation [29,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical and Photochemical properties of CeCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectrum of CeCDs clearly exhibit two characteristic bands at 240 nm and 348 nm, which can be ascribed to the electron transitions from π–π* of the aromatic sp 2 domains and n–π* of C = O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1d). Such CeCDs mainly emitted intense blue-green fluorescence when excited within the wavelength range from 300 to 420 nm, with the best emission obtained at 459 nm under 366 nm excitation, displaying excitation-independent emission (Fig. S4). Notably, the as-prepared CeCDs possess a high absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 79.7 % ( Fig. 2a), facilitating the formation of photogeneration charge carriers [31,32]. In addition, the lifetime of CeCDs was about 14.40 ns (Fig. S5), which allowed the excited CeCDs convert energy to oxygen to produce ROS. Furthermore, the photocurrent transient measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed to evaluate photosensitization activity of CeCDs. As shown in Fig. 2b&c, compared with CDs, stronger photocurrent response signals and smaller Nyquist plot radius were recorded, revealing the enhanced photo-induced charge separation efficiency and reduced charge transfer resistance of CeCDs. A series of experiments suggested that cerium doping in CDs could effectively capture the photogenerated electrons and holes and increase the charge transfer to promote ROS generation.

The ROS generation ability of CeCDs under light irradiation was investigated using DCFH-DA as the indicator, which can be readily oxidized to 2,7-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) by ROS and exhibits bright green fluorescence. The results showed that DCFH-DA treated with CDs demonstrated almost no fluorescence, while remarkable fluorescence signals were detected in CeCDs group, verifying the ROS generation ability of CeCDs. Moreover, more ROS was produced upon the light irradiation, confirming light-driven photodynamic activity enhancement of CeCDs. And the amount of ROS production was closely correlated with the irradiation time and concentration of CeCDs used (Fig. 2d and Fig. S6). Compared with typical photosensitizer Methylene Blue, CeCDs has better photostability and higher ROS generation capabilities (Fig. S7).

Because reactive oxygen species (ROS) including superoxide anions (O2·-), hydroxyl radical (OH·), and singlet oxygen (1O2), to identify the distinctive role of different ROS species, ESR spin trapping experiments were employed to evaluate the types of generated ROS. As shown in Fig. 2e, under light irradiation, the CeCDs exhibited much stronger specific EPR signals of 1O2 and OH· than that in the dark. Note that characteristic peak for O2·- did not appear in CeCDs aqueous solution without exposure to light, indicating that light irradiation not only improve ROS generation efficiencies, but also related to the type of ROS. And the formation of the valence-band holes (h +) is beneficial to produce ROS during irradiation.
Taken together, CeCDs could act as an effective photosensitizer to generate ROS upon white light irradiation, demonstrating the great potential for photodynamic therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of CeCDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro antibacterial performance of CeCDs against four bacterial strains including S. aureus, E. coli, ESBL-Ec, and MRSA were further evaluated comprehensively based on the bacteria counting method. E. coli and ESBL-Ec were used as representative strain of Gram-negative bacteria, while S. aureus and MRSA belonging to Gram-positive bacteria, which ESBL-Ec and MRSA are examples of corresponding clinical drug-resistant bacteria. All bacteria were cultured with and without CeCDs in light and dark, and then plated on LB solid media to visualize the viable cells. As illustrated in Fig. 3, CeCDs shows intrinsic superior antibacterial ability toward the four bacterial species. With the increase concentration of CeCDs, the survival rates decreased significantly. Specifically, the survival rates of E. coli, S. aureus, ESBL-Ec, and MRSA were 2.84 %, 16.9 %, 28.49 %, and 98.47 %, respectively, with the treatment of CeCDs (40 μg/mL). And the antibacterial efficiencies of CeCDs is higher against Gram-negative bacteria than Gram-positive bacteria. Notably, the antibacterial effect was obviously improved by light irradiation. The low concentration of CeCDs (20 μg/mL) treatment effectively eliminated E. coli after light irradiation, indicating the great enhancement of light on antibacterial activity. The survival rates of E. coli, S. aureus, ESBL-Ec, and MRSA were 0.27 %, 1.06 %, 8.13 %, and 55.93 %, respectively, with the treatment of CeCDs (40 μg/mL) under white light irradiation, revealing that CeCDs possess broad-spectrum antibacterial activity. In addition, MRSA bacterial survival with 100 μg/mL CeCDs under white light was below 10 % (Fig. S8). The irradiation time and light intensity both have vital influence on the bactericidal effect (Fig. S9). When light was applied for 2 h and the light intensity was controlled at 3613 lux, the colony number of E. coli were significantly reduced. As a control sample, all bacterial strains kept their survival rates under the same light irradiation in the absence of CeCDs, confirming that the reason for bacteria death was indeed attributed to CeCDs plus light irradiation (Fig. S10). More importantly, with the assistance of white light, multidrug-resistant bacteria can also be killed efficiently by CeCDs, especially for ESBL-Ec strain. Furthermore, we taken other clinical isolates of ESBL-Ec and MRSA strains, isolated from the patient’s urine or sputum that are susceptibilities to a series of antibiotics, to evaluate the antibacterial capabilities of CeCDs (Fig. S11). The results showed that light-assisted CeCDs treatment strategy also possessed an obvious bactericidal effect on these hospital-derived MRSA and ESBL-Ec, indicating their great potential to fight off common bacterial resistance. In contrast, no reduced bacterial cells were observed in the presence of CDs and irradiation, indicating that CDs did not hold any antibacterial ability by itself and highlighting the central function of the Ce doping in maintaining the antibacterial ability (Fig. S12). Since bacteria can acquire antibiotic resistance, it is not clear whether the bacteria would develop resistance to CeCDs. Therefore, a drug-resistance simulation study, over nine cycles, similar antibacterial efficacy was present, showing that ESBL-Ec and MRSA did not develop any resistance to CeCDs (Fig. S13). Strikingly, the excellent antibacterial property of the CeCDs were still maintained even exposure to mercury lamps for 3 h, which showed a good stability of the CeCDs (Fig. S14). The commonly used antibiotics sulfadiazine (SDZ) or ampicillin (AMP) have lower antimicrobial activity in combination with CeCDs than one antimicrobial substance alone, suggesting the potential for better therapeutic outcomes when CeCDs are used in combination with antibiotics (Fig. S15).

A series of experiments proved that CeCDs possessed excellent broad-spectrum activity against bacteria that are clinically relevant, especially under white light irradiation, implying that CeCDs could be served as an efficient photosensitizer for photodynamic therapy.

Antibacterial mechanism of CeCDs was further systematically evaluated. The rapid and efficient generation of ROS such as O2·–, 1O2, ·OH after irradiation, which can kill bacteria via oxidative damage, was the key antibacterial mechanism for CeCDs. Cell-permeable dihydroethidium (DHE) is employed as an indicator of intracellular ROS formation, which can be oxidized to the highly fluorescent product. S. aureus incubated with DHE alone given negligible intracellular background fluorescence, and prompt appearance of bright red fluorescence in bacteria was observed upon addition of CeCDs (Fig. S16). The fluorescence intensity was calculated, showing nearly 3-fold enhancement compared to the control group, which was increased 25-fold in the presence of light excitation. At the same time, after CDs treatment the internal ROS of the bacteria will also produce a small amount of ROS (Fig. S17), which is not enough to kill the bacteria. Subsequently, we investigated if ROS scavenger could protect cells from CeCDs-mediated oxidative damage. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) significantly decreased the antibacterial effect of CeCDs treatment in concentration dependence, suggesting that the oxidative stress was the governing mechanism for the antibacterial activity, especially under light irradiation ( Fig. 4d). Furthermore, dead bacteria were selectively stained with propidium iodide (PI) which can only enter through the damaged membrane. The strong intracellular red fluorescence was clearly seen on all bacteria including MDR stains after exposure to CeCDs while no obvious fluorescence signal could be detected in the saline treatment group, indicating that the damage of bacterial cell membrane (Fig. 4a&b). Similar results were also confirmed by the corresponding SEM images, where CeCDs induced the holes, wrinkles, and shrinkage on the surface of bacterial membrane. The control groups even in the presence of light irradiation still maintained their intact and smooth membranes. Moreover, the bacterial morphology in the CeCDs treated group was not as full as that in the control group, indicating that the cell wall was also damaged. It has been shown by functional group passivation experiments that oxygen-containing functional groups, such as -COOH, -OH, and CO, are the main contributors to the negative charge exhibited by CeCDs (Fig. S18 and Fig. S19) [33]. Although these negatively charged CeCDs may not exhibit strong bacterial adhesion, they can still cover the outer surface of bacteria and indirectly exert toxicity on the bacteria cell due to biocontainment with the growth medium [34]. Damage to bacterial cell membranes and cell walls by CeCDs is attributed to the combined action of surface oxygen-containing functional groups and ROS. In addition, considering the intrinsic excellent fluorescence characteristics of CeCDs, the binding of CeCDs to bacteria cells was further investigated. The CLSM images in Fig. 4c displayed that ESBL-Ec and MRSA can be labeled by the CeCDs effectively, demonstrating that CeCDs can interacted with the bacteria after incubation. Meanwhile, CeCDs may enter the bacterial interior, bind to bacterial DNA, and disrupt its structure (Fig. S20 and Table S1).

Besides, E. coli-derived LPS was also carried out to evaluate the role of CeCDs against the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria. It is well-known that Gram-positive bacteria do not have a protective membrane layer composed of LPS compare to Gram-negative bacteria [29]. As shown in Fig. 4e, the antibacterial activity of CeCDs against E. coli displayed a concentration-dependent with the exogenous addition of LPS, suggesting that the competition binding of LPS to CeCDs could reduce antibacterial activity significantly. Same result has been observed in the drug resistant bacteria strain ESBL-Ec (Fig. S21). Hydrodynamic diameter distribution and thermogravimetric analysis （TGA） further validates the combination of CeCDs and LPS (Fig. S22 and Fig. S23). For this reason, the antibacterial effects from CeCDs could be better against Gram-negative bacteria than Gram-positive bacteria, which was consistent with above experimental result (Fig. 3).

Additionally, biofilms, an extracellular polysaccharide matrix, consisted of microcolonies encapsulated, is a great challenge in the management of bacterial infection [35]. Therefore, we further explored the biofilm eradication property of CeCDs based on the crystal violet (CV) staining method (Fig. 4f). As expected, CeCDs could inhibit the formation of biofilms in the E. coli and S. aureus effectively. It should be noted that higher inhibition level was observed under light irradiation. The antibiofilm activity appeared be correlated with the ROS because the effect of the CeCDs on the biofilms was significantly reduced following ROS scavenging by NAC.

Taken together, the broad-spectrum antibacterial ability for CeCDs benefit from the small size to penetrate the bacterial membrane, bacterial cell wall and accumulate in the bacteria. A large generation of ROS induced the rupture of the bacterial membrane and inhibit the formation of biofilms is the main antibacterial mechanism for CeCDs, which can kill the bacterial cells effectively. CeCDs, as a robust antibacterial agent, provide promising therapeutic option of biomedicine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in antibacterial therapy in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of CeCDs was firstly assessed by hemolysis test (Fig. S24). As illustrated, CeCDs displayed a negligible hemolysis ratio (2.94 %) even at high concentration of CeCDs (up to 400 μg/mL), indicating that CeCDs hold excellent blood compatibility. Furthermore, cytotoxicity of CeCDs was also investigated toward mammalian cells was evaluated with HepG2 and CEM cells (Fig. S25). The good cell viability of both HepG2 and CEM cells in the dark was maintained at a concentration of 40 μg/mL of CeCDs. Upon light irradiation, the cell survival rates of the tested cells were higher than 65 % at the same CeCDs concentration, implying the good biocompatibility of CeCDs.

Subsequently, the in vivo antibacterial effect of CeCDs was investigated by a skin wound healing model ( Fig. 5a). The infected mice with CeCDs treatment on the application of light had the smallest scab with intact epidermal layer, whereas the wound in the control group (saline) still showed a large scab (Fig. 5b). And the area of the wound was much smaller compared to those treated with saline or CeCDs without irradiation, suggesting that CeCDs with light irradiation can significantly promote the healing process (Fig. 5c). Fig. 5d presented the schematics of the wound healing process. Notably, no obvious inflammation toward skin tissue was observed for the CeCDs exposure even at continuous use of CeCDs on the mice skin. Furthermore, the amounts of bacteria in the infected sites were calculated through the colony counting at the end of therapeutic process. There were no bacteria survived after CeCDs treatment with light irradiation compared to the saline groups, while only a few survival bacteria appeared in CeCDs group, exhibiting the outstanding in vivo antibacterial performance of CeCDs. The wound tissues were collected and evaluated by histological analyses on day 13 (Fig. 5e). Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining results indicated that the CeCDs with light irradiation groups exhibited more hair follicle and sweat gland formation, less inflammatory cell infiltration, and basically reconstruction of the epithelial tissue compared with other treatment groups. To further evaluate the potential systemic toxicity, main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) with different treatment were collected and stained (Fig. S26). The results revealed that there was no obvious histological damage, demonstrating the good biocompatibility of CeCDs.

Overall, the above results suggested that CeCDs treatment upon light could effectively combat the bacteria infection in vivo, holding great application prospects in the prevention of wound infection and promoting wound healing.

In conclusion, we prepared CeCDs to identify their photodynamic antibacterial effect. The obtained CeCDs with high quantum yield were well-dispersed and stable in aqueous solution. With the help of white light irradiation, they can kill MDR bacteria efficiently by producing more ROS (including ·OH, O2·–, and 1O2) for damaging the biofilm and bacterial membrane, which did not suffer from the resistance. On the other hand, CeCDs showed great biocompatibility and impressive therapeutic efficacy for Gram-negative bacteria infection. Our finds provide new ideas for tackling antibiotic resistance and this white light-activated CeCDs might be useful and promising for developing innovative antibacterial agents.

Lijun Zhang: Writing – original draft, Resources, Conceptualization. Na Chen: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lin Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Mengzhen Wang: Validation, Formal analysis. Lei Zhan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Chengzhi Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21976144 and 22134005), and Chongqing Talents Program for Outstanding Scientists (cstc2021ycjh-bgzxm0179) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.114086.
